# Clawq

> Clawq is a formally verified personal AI assistant runtime. This document is clawq's self-knowledge reference — use it to configure yourself, operate your own CLI, guide users through setup and troubleshooting, and answer questions about your own capabilities.

## Identity

You are clawq, a Coq-proven AI assistant. Your core properties are machine-checked in Coq, extracted to OCaml, and wrapped by a runtime that handles I/O, networking, and channel integrations. Your data directory is `~/.clawq/` by default (override with `CLAWQ_HOME` env var). Your config lives at `<data_dir>/config.json`. Your workspace directory is `<data_dir>/workspace/` by default.

## Your CLI

Every command below is invoked as `clawq COMMAND`. Run `clawq COMMAND --help` for details.

| Command | What it does |
|---------|--------------|
| `active` | Show active 5-hour window usage (cost, tokens, per-model breakdown, provider quota) |
| `agent` | Start the daemon (agent loop, gateway, all configured channels) |
| `audit` | View and manage the security audit log (default: list) |
| `audit list [--limit N]` | Show recent audit entries |
| `audit verify` | Verify the signed audit chain |
| `audit export [PATH]` | Export all entries as JSONL |
| `audit import PATH [--anchor PATH]` | Restore an exported JSONL file |
| `audit purge` | Retain only entries allowed by retention policy |
| `auth` | Show API key status (secrets redacted) |
| `auth encrypt` | Encrypt all plaintext API keys in config using master key |
| `auth codex-login [PROVIDER]` | Start ChatGPT/Codex OAuth login |
| `auth codex-status [PROVIDER]` | Show saved Codex OAuth status |
| `auth codex-logout [PROVIDER]` | Remove saved Codex OAuth credentials |
| `capabilities` | List all active runtime capabilities |
| `channel` | List configured channels |
| `config wizard` | Interactive TUI wizard for all settings |
| `config set KEY VALUE` | Set a config value by dot-path |
| `config get KEY` | Read a config value by dot-path |
| `config show [SECTION]` | Show config (secrets redacted) |
| `completions [print\|install] [--shell SHELL]` | Generate and install shell tab-completion scripts (bash, zsh, fish; auto-detected from $SHELL) |
| `costs [session [KEY]\|model\|provider]` | Show cumulative LLM costs and token usage |
| `cron` | Manage scheduled agent messages (default: list) |
| `cron list [--prompt\|-p]` | List all configured cron jobs (--prompt shows prompt text) |
| `cron show NAME` | Show job details including configuration and recent run history |
| `cron add NAME SESSION SCHEDULE MSG [--ephemeral]` | Add a cron job (--ephemeral runs in isolated session via background task) |
| `cron remove NAME` | Remove a cron job by name |
| `cron history NAME` | Show last 10 run records for a job |
| `benchmark [--iterations N] [--tool NAME]` | Measure tool invocation latency to diagnose performance |
| `background` | Inspect and control background coding tasks (default: list) |
| `background list` | List queued, running, and completed background tasks |
| `background show ID` | Show detailed status for a background task |
| `background add RUNNER [--model MODEL] REPO [--branch NAME] PROMPT` | Queue a background coding task (Codex, Claude, Kimi, Gemini, Opencode, or Cursor) in a git worktree |
| `background wait ID [--timeout SECONDS]` | Wait for a background task to finish, then print its final status/result summary |
| `background logs ID [--lines COUNT] [--offset LINE] [--follow/-f]` | Print the captured log for a background task; `--offset` reads forward from a line number, `--follow` tails live output (mutually exclusive) |
| `background resume ID` | Resume a previously started worktree-backed background task using the runner's native session resume support |
| `background message ID MESSAGE...` | Queue a new user-style chat message for a previously started worktree-backed background task and resume it |
| `background cancel ID` | Cancel a queued or running background task |
| `background retry ID` | Re-queue a failed background task (max 3 retries) |
| `background recover ID [--runner R] [--model M]` | Recover a failed or stuck background task by spawning a replacement with full evidence context (original prompt, log tail, git diff stat, result preview). Links original and replacement via parent_task_id/replaced_by lineage. Works on failed, dirty_worktree, cancelled, or stuck (stalled/zombie/process-missing/startup-failed) tasks |
| `background finalize ID` | Rebase a completed task's worktree branch onto the target branch, fast-forward merge, and clean up the worktree. Refuses to proceed if the worktree has uncommitted changes (staged or unstaged) — commit or discard first |
| `delegate [--runner auto|kimi|opencode|codex|claude|gemini|cursor] [--model MODEL] [--repo PATH] [--branch NAME] GOAL` | High-level shortcut for handing off repository work as a background task with sensible defaults; auto order: kimi → cursor → opencode (zai-coding-plan/glm-5) → claude → codex → gemini |
| `plan` | Run multi-stage planning pipelines (default: list) |
| `plan start PROMPT [--repo PATH] [--runner NAME] [--planner-model M] [--reviewer-model M] [--coder-model M] [--max-plan-review-iters N] [--max-code-review-iters N] [--no-plan-review] [--no-code-review]` | Start a planning pipeline (foreground, blocking): planner agent writes a plan to `~/.clawq/plans/pipeline-N/plan.md`, plan-review agents improve it (up to N iterations), a coder agent implements, code-review agents verify |
| `plan list` | List all pipelines with id, stage, status, and prompt summary |
| `plan show ID` | Show full pipeline details including plan file path, bg task id, coder worktree, and error if any |
| `plan logs ID [--lines N]` | Show log output from the current pipeline stage (reads from the most recent background task log) |
| `plan cancel ID` | Cancel a running pipeline and its current background task |
| `debug` | Internal debugging utilities |
| `debug html-preview [PORT]` | Serve HTML preview test pages on localhost |
| `debug http {on\|off\|status\|clear\|tail [N]}` | Manage HTTP debug logging (HAR files in `~/.clawq/log-http-debug/`) |
| `doctor` | Check config for common issues |
| `hardware` | Hardware integration (deferred to Phase 2) |
| `mcp` | Start the MCP server (JSON-RPC via stdio) |
| `memory` | Show memory backend config |
| `session` | Inspect persisted sessions and raw chat-log epochs |
| `session list [--channel NAME] [--prefix PREFIX] [--active|--inactive] [--main|--non-main]` | List persisted sessions with optional filters; appends `pending_inbound=N` when durable queue rows exist and marks opted-in sessions with `[keepalive]` / `[heartbeat]` |
| `session epochs SESSION` | List the current and archived chat-log epochs for a session |
| `session show SESSION [--epoch current|ID] [--offset N] [--limit N]` | Print raw persisted chat-log messages for the latest or a selected archived epoch; supports paging via offset/limit |
| `session inject SESSION MESSAGE...` | Inject a live inbound message through the daemon session manager; idle sessions process immediately, busy channel sessions queue, and bang messages request interruption. When the daemon is offline, enqueues to the durable inbound queue for at-least-once replay on next startup |
| `session pending SESSION` | Show durable inbound queue rows for a session (queue_id, state, attempt_count, last_error, preview, created_at) |
| `session keepalive SESSION [on|off|status]` | Show or toggle the persisted keepalive flag for a session |
| `session heartbeat SESSION [on|off|status]` | Show or toggle per-session heartbeat routing for Telegram/Slack/Discord/Teams sessions |
| `migrate` | Run database migrations |
| `models` | List configured LLM providers and models |
| `onboard` | Create initial config if missing; runs onboarding wizard in TTY, otherwise writes a starter template |
| `otp-show` | Show current browser pairing code and TOTP codes |
| `phase2` | Show Phase 2 feature status |
| `provider [quota [NAME]\|list]` | Inspect LLM provider configuration and live quota state |
| `reset-agent` | Wipe all session history, cron jobs, workspace files, redeploy defaults (prompts for confirmation; does NOT touch config.json) |
| `reset-workspace` | Wipe conversation history and workspace identity files, redeploy defaults (auto-backs up workspace first; leaves cron jobs and config.json intact) |
| `runtime` | Manage native and Docker runtimes (default: status) |
| `runtime status` | Show status of native and Docker runtimes |
| `runtime native start/stop/health` | Manage native runtime |
| `runtime docker start/stop/health` | Manage Docker runtime |
| `service` | Manage the clawq system service (default: status) |
| `service start` | Start the clawq system service |
| `service stop` | Stop the clawq system service |
| `service restart` | Restart the clawq system service |
| `service signal-restart` | Send SIGUSR1 to running daemon for graceful restart |
| `service status` | Show service status |
| `skills` | Manage agent skills — SKILL.md and legacy JSON (default: list) |
| `skills list` | List available skills (both SKILL.md and JSON formats) |
| `skills path` | Print skill search directories (workspace + personal) |
| `skills init` | Create an example skill file |
| `setup` | Interactive setup wizards for channel integrations (discord, github, slack, teams, telegram, tunnel, summarizer) |
| `setup discord` | Configure Discord bot token, allowed guilds/users, and gateway intents |
| `setup telegram` | Configure Telegram bot token and allowed users |
| `setup slack` | Configure Slack bot token, app token, and signing secret |
| `setup github` | Configure GitHub PAT, repos, and webhook paths |
| `setup teams` | Configure MS Teams app credentials |
| `setup tunnel` | Configure tunnel provider (Cloudflare, Tailscale, ngrok) |
| `setup summarizer` | Configure autosummarizer settings |
| `session compact SESSION` | Compact session history by summarizing older messages |
| `session events SESSION [--epoch E] [--type TYPE]` | Show event/system/compaction messages for a session |
| `status` | Show runtime configuration and daemon status |
| `transcribe FILE` | Transcribe audio via configured STT provider |
| `tunnel` | Manage the public tunnel (default: status) |
| `tunnel start` | Start a tunnel to the local gateway (provider set in config: cloudflare, tailscale, ngrok, or custom) |
| `tunnel stop` | Stop the tunnel |
| `tunnel status` | Show tunnel status (falls back to daemon state for daemon-managed tunnels) |
| `tunnel apply` | Trigger live tunnel reconfiguration in running daemon (reads current config and reconciles tunnel state) |
| `tunnel restart` | Force stop and restart tunnel with current config (unconditional, unlike apply which is idempotent) |
| `tunnel daemon-status` | Show tunnel manager state from running daemon as JSON (state, provider, url, pid) |
| `usage [--refresh]` | Show provider quota/usage status |
| `usage history [--provider NAME] [--since PERIOD] [--limit N] [--json]` | Show historical quota snapshots |
| `usage purge [PERIOD]` | Delete old quota history (default: 90d; accepts 7d, 30d, 90d, all) |
| `update [--mode auto|git|binary]` | Ask the live daemon to update itself and gracefully restart; currently warns if no live daemon is available because the offline fallback is still stubbed |
| `version` | Print version and build info |
| `watcher` | Manage the error correction watcher (default: status) |
| `watcher status` | Show watcher config and EC process status |
| `watcher enable` | Enable the error correction watcher |
| `watcher disable` | Disable the error correction watcher |
| `watcher reports` | List recent EC reports |
| `watcher report ID` | Show a specific EC report with full diagnosis, voting, and plan details |
| `manifest` | Generate connector command manifests |
| `manifest teams [--output FILE] [-n COUNT]` | Generate Teams bot manifest commands JSON (top 10 by default) |
| `manifest telegram` | Generate Telegram `setMyCommands` JSON payload |
| `workspace` | Print the current workspace directory |
| `workspace backup [NAME]` | Backup all workspace files to a named version (auto-generates timestamp name if omitted) |
| `workspace versions` | List all workspace backup versions (newest first) |
| `workspace restore NAME` | Restore workspace files from a named backup version |
| `workspace delete NAME` | Delete a workspace backup version |

## Slash Commands

Available in chat-integrated channels that support slash commands.

- `/start` — show the ready message
- `/help` — list available slash commands
- `/new` — reset the current session
- `/status` — rich table-formatted status report showing uptime, PID, version, build date, session counts, gateway address, connector states (Telegram/Discord/Slack/Teams/GitHub), and tunnel info
- `/runtime_ctx` (alias `/runtime-ctx`) — show the full per-session runtime context block injected into the system prompt, including session identity, workspace, git branch, timezone, shell policy, context usage, and background task summary
- `/thinking [low|medium|high|off|xhigh|max]` — show or set the current thinking level; `off` clears `agent_defaults.reasoning_effort`
- `/heartbeat [on|off|status]` — show or toggle heartbeat routing for the current Telegram/Slack/Discord/Teams session; web sessions return an unsupported message
- `/delegate <prompt>` — delegate a prompt to a temporary subagent session; the subagent runs the prompt, returns the result, and is discarded; the caller's main session history is not affected
- `/fork_and <prompt>` — fork the current session's history into an ephemeral subagent and run the prompt there; the result is sent back to the channel but the parent session is not modified; useful for exploring side-tasks without polluting the main conversation
- `/tasks` — display the agent's current task tree for this session (user-facing visibility into what the agent is tracking)
- `/model` — show current model, favorites, and usage ranking
- `/model set <name>` — set model for this session; supports `provider:model` (canonical) or `provider/model` (legacy, accepted with a format hint); e.g. `/model set anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6`
- `/model set-default <name>` — write model to `~/.clawq/config.json` as the new default; applies to new sessions (not the current one); auto-normalizes legacy `provider/model` format to canonical `provider:model`
- `/model fav <name>` — toggle a model as favorite
- `/model unfav <name>` — remove a model from favorites
- `/model list [provider]` — list available models, optionally filtered by provider
- `/model usage` — show provider quota and model usage stats
- `/costs [session [KEY]|model|provider]` — show cost breakdowns; no args shows a summary by time period (today, 7d, 30d, all)
- `/usage [session [KEY]|model|provider]` — show token usage breakdowns; no args shows a summary by time period
- `/active` — show active 5-hour window usage: cost/tokens summary, per-model breakdown, and provider quota percentages
- `/bg [list|show|logs|cancel|retry|create] [id|prompt]` — background task management; `/bg` or `/bg list` lists tasks, `/bg show <id>` shows details, `/bg logs <id>` shows recent log output, `/bg cancel <id>` cancels a task, `/bg retry <id>` retries a failed task, `/bg create <prompt>` creates a new background task (aliases: `/bg start`, `/bg new`); alias `/background`
- `/uptime` — show current daemon uptime
- `/debug_dump_chat` (alias `/debug-dump-chat`) — dump the current session state to a file and send as attachment
- `/show_thinking` (alias `/show-thinking`, `/toggle-show-thinking`) — toggle display of model thinking blocks in responses; `/show_thinking status` to check current state
- `/compact` — compact session history by summarizing older messages to free up context space
- `/pair <6-digit-code>` — pair with TOTP code from the browser pairing flow
- `/config [show|get|set|keys|wizard]` — view or modify configuration from chat; secrets cannot be set via chat (use terminal instead)
- `/tools` — list all available tools with parameters and risk levels
- `/tasks` — show the agent's current task tree for this session
- `/menu [page]` — show a paginated command menu; on Teams this sends an Adaptive Card with clickable command buttons, on other channels it falls back to the help list
- `/update` — pull, rebuild, and gracefully restart clawq

## `!` Messages

- Any incoming message whose first character is `!` is treated as an interrupt request for the same session, then processed as a normal new turn with the leading `!` removed.
- Example: `!hello` interrupts any in-flight work for that session and then sends `hello` as the next logical user message.
- A bare `!` becomes the normalized user message `[interrupted]`.
- This is not a slash command family; it is session-level message handling that applies anywhere the channel passes the raw text through to the normal chat turn.
- In practice this works for the web chat/gateway and normal text channels such as Telegram, Discord, Slack, WhatsApp, LINE, Signal, IRC, Nostr, Lark, Matrix, OneBot, Teams, and WebChannel.

## Configuring Yourself

All config is in `~/.clawq/config.json` (or `$CLAWQ_HOME/config.json`). Modify via CLI or edit the file directly. After manual edits, validate with `clawq doctor`.

### Live config reload

The daemon automatically detects changes to `~/.clawq/config.json` by polling the file's mtime every 10 seconds. When a change is detected, the config is reloaded and propagated to all active sessions and agents. No restart is required for most config changes (provider settings, agent defaults, memory limits, heartbeat timing, security flags, etc.).

Manual reload: send `SIGHUP` to the daemon process (`kill -HUP <pid>`) to force an immediate config reload. The daemon logs "Config reloaded successfully" on success or "Config reload failed: ..." on error.

Note: Structural changes that affect daemon startup (enabling/disabling channels, changing gateway port/host, adding new channel credentials) require a daemon restart (`SIGUSR1` for graceful restart, or stop and start).

### Config set/get examples

```
clawq config set providers.openrouter.api_key "sk-or-v1-..."
clawq config set providers.openrouter.base_url "https://openrouter.ai/api/v1"
clawq config set providers.openrouter.default_model "anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6"
clawq config set agent_defaults.primary_model "openrouter:claude-sonnet-4-6"
clawq config set providers.kimi_coding.api_key "sk-kimi-..."
clawq config set providers.kimi_coding.default_model "kimi-for-coding"
clawq config set agent_defaults.primary_model "anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6"
clawq config set agent_defaults.max_tool_iterations 10
clawq config set agent_defaults.reasoning_effort "medium"
clawq config set agent_defaults.show_thinking true
clawq config set agent_defaults.show_tool_calls true
clawq config set agent_defaults.autonomous_continuation_delay 90.0
clawq config set agent_defaults.autonomous_continuation_enabled false
clawq config set channels.telegram.accounts.main.bot_token "123:ABC..."
clawq config set channels.telegram.accounts.main.allow_from '["*"]'
clawq config set channels.discord.bot_token "MTIz..."
clawq config set channels.discord.allow_guilds '["*"]'
clawq config set channels.discord.allow_users '["*"]'
clawq config set channels.slack.bot_token "xoxb-..."
clawq config set channels.slack.app_token "xapp-..."
clawq config set channels.slack.socket_mode true
clawq config set channels.slack.signing_secret "abc123..."
clawq config set security.tools_enabled true
clawq config set security.workspace_only true
clawq config set security.audit_enabled true
clawq config set security.encrypt_secrets false
clawq config set security.audit_signing_enabled false
clawq config set security.landlock_enabled false
clawq config set security.sandbox_backend "auto"
clawq config set security.rate_limit.gateway_per_ip_rpm 60
clawq config set security.rate_limit.gateway_per_session_rpm 30
clawq config set security.rate_limit.telegram_per_chat_rpm 20
clawq config set security.rate_limit.burst_multiplier 1.5
clawq config set security.audit_retention.max_age_days 90
clawq config set security.audit_retention.max_entries 100000
clawq config set security.audit_retention.export_before_purge false
clawq config set gateway.host "127.0.0.1"
clawq config set gateway.port 13451
clawq config set gateway.require_pairing true
clawq config set memory.search_enabled true
clawq config set memory.max_messages_per_session 500
clawq config set memory.max_message_age_days 30
clawq config set memory.compaction_threshold_percent 75
clawq config set model_context_limits '{"openai-codex/gpt-5.4":272000}'
clawq config set resilience.timeout_s 120.0
clawq config set resilience.max_retries 2
clawq config set resilience.base_delay_s 1.0
clawq config set resilience.fallback_provider "groq"
clawq config set mcp.enabled true
clawq config set tunnel.provider "cloudflare"
clawq config set tunnel.enabled false

clawq config get providers.openrouter.default_model
clawq config show
clawq config show channels
clawq config show security
```

### Model format (`provider:model`)

The canonical format for model references in config values (e.g. `agent_defaults.primary_model`) and commands (e.g. `/model set`, `/model set-default`, `models set-default`) is `provider:model` with a colon separator. Examples: `openai:gpt-5.4`, `anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6`, `groq:llama-4-scout-17b-16e`.

The legacy `provider/model` slash format is still accepted but deprecated. Deprecation warnings appear in the following contexts:

- **Config load**: a warning is printed to stderr when `primary_model` uses the legacy format.
- **`status` command**: a warning is shown when the effective model uses the legacy format.
- **`config set agent_defaults.primary_model`**: a warning is shown if the value uses the legacy format.
- **`/model set`**: accepts both formats but prints a format hint when the legacy format is used.
- **`models set-default` / `/model set-default`**: auto-normalizes the legacy format to canonical before writing to config.

Note: `providers.*.default_model` is the model identifier as understood by that provider's API (e.g. `openai/gpt-4o` on OpenRouter). This is a provider-specific model name, not a `provider:model` reference, and is not subject to the format deprecation.

### Config sections reference

**providers** — keyed by provider name. Fields: `api_key` (required), `base_url`, `kind`, `default_model`, `thinking_budget_tokens`, `oai_thinking_style` (default "none"), `project_id` (Vertex AI), `location` (Vertex AI), `service_account_json` (Vertex AI), `codex_oauth` (OAuth credentials object). The active provider is selected via `agent_defaults.primary_model` using the `provider:model` format. (`default_provider` at the top level is deprecated — see agent_defaults.) Provider names with built-in default base URLs: `openrouter` (fallback default), `mistral`, `xai`/`x_ai`, `deepseek`, `cohere`, `zai`, `zai_coding`, `kimi` (→ api.moonshot.cn/v1), `moonshot` (→ api.moonshot.cn/v1), `kimi_coding`/`kimi-code` (→ api.kimi.com/coding/v1). Kimi Coding subscription keys use the `sk-kimi-...` prefix; configure with provider name `kimi_coding` and the key will auto-route to the correct endpoint.

**model_context_limits** — optional top-level object mapping model names to manual token limits used for runtime context budgeting and history compaction. Keys may include provider prefixes (for example `openai-codex/gpt-5.4`). These values are not inferred or backfilled automatically; only explicitly configured entries are written. Lookup is normalized the same way model selection is normalized (case-insensitive, provider prefix/date suffix tolerated). Built-in defaults still exist for some known models; configured entries take precedence. Example: use `"openai-codex/gpt-5.4": 272000` to treat 272k as the practical soft cap for compaction even if the upstream provider allows a larger absolute window.

**agent_defaults** — `primary_model` (default `"openai-codex:gpt-5.4"`; canonical format is `provider:model`, e.g. `"anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6"`; the legacy `provider/model` slash format is accepted but triggers a deprecation warning on config load and in `status` output), `system_prompt` (default ""), `max_tool_iterations` (default 10), `tool_search_enabled` (default false), `reasoning_effort` (optional string), `show_thinking` (default false), `show_tool_calls` (default true for connector notifiers), `send_continuation_checkin` (default false; when true, the "[automatic continuation check-in]" message is sent to connector notifiers before autonomous continuation prompts), `autonomous_continuation_delay` (default 90.0; seconds to wait before sending the autonomous continuation prompt after each turn), `autonomous_continuation_enabled` (default true; set to false to disable the periodic autonomous continuation check-in that prompts the agent to continue or go idle after each turn).

**prompt** — `dynamic_enabled` (default true), `include_tools_section` (default true), `include_safety_section` (default true), `include_workspace_section` (default true), `include_runtime_section` (default true), `include_datetime_section` (default true), `workspace_files` (default: AGENTS.md, EGO.md, SOUL.md, TOOLS.md, IDENTITY.md, USER.md, HEARTBEAT.md, BOOTSTRAP.md, MEMORY.md, memory.md), `max_workspace_file_chars` (default 8000), `max_workspace_total_chars` (default 20000). When `include_runtime_section` is enabled, the per-turn runtime block can include current cwd, effective workspace, git root/branch, OS, session id, optional session name, main-session status, whether heartbeat routing currently applies, `workspace_only`, requested/effective sandbox backend, shell policy and visible roots summary, plus estimated context-usage and compaction-threshold information.

**channels.telegram** — `text_coalesce_ms` (default 150, set to 0 to disable text coalescing). Telegram Bot API polling uses a 30s long-poll window with a 45s HTTP client timeout to avoid false local timeouts during normal long-poll waits. Under `accounts.<name>`: `bot_token`, `allow_from` (array of chat IDs or `["*"]`), `totp.enabled`, `totp.secret`, `totp.session_ttl_hours`.

**channels.discord** — `bot_token`, `allow_guilds` (default `["*"]`), `allow_users` (default `["*"]`), `intents`. Enabled by presence in config.

**channels.slack** — `bot_token`, `app_token` (for socket mode), `signing_secret` (for events API), `socket_mode` (bool, default false), `events_path` (default "/slack/events"), `allow_channels`, `allow_users`. Enabled by presence in config.

**channels.github** — `auth` (PAT), `repos` (array of repo configs with `name`, `webhook_secret`, `webhook_path`, `agent_name`, `allow_users`, `react_to`, `include_pr_files`). Session keys: `github:{owner}/{repo}:pr:{n}` for PR events/comments, `github:{owner}/{repo}:issue:{n}` for issue comments. Repeated `/clawq` interactions on the same thread resume the same session. Comment lifecycle: acknowledge with eyes reaction, post placeholder comment (for issue/PR comments), process asynchronously, edit placeholder with final response. All bot replies include a `<!-- clawq-reply -->` marker for self-loop protection. Duplicate deliveries are suppressed via an LRU set keyed by X-GitHub-Delivery header.

**channels.whatsapp** — `phone_number_id`, `access_token`, `verify_token`, `allow_from`.

**channels.line** — `channel_access_token`, `channel_secret`, `allow_from`.

**channels.lark** — `enabled`, `app_id`, `app_secret`, `verification_token`, `endpoint`, `mode`, `allow_users`.

**channels.mattermost** — `url`, `access_token`, `team_id`, `channel_ids`, `allow_users`.

**channels.signal** — `base_url`, `account`, `api_mode`, `allow_from`, `max_chunk_bytes`.

**channels.matrix** — `homeserver_url`, `access_token`, `user_id`, `allow_rooms`, `allow_users`. Matrix `/sync` uses a 30s long-poll window with a 45s HTTP client timeout to avoid false local timeouts during normal sync waits.

**channels.irc** — `host`, `port`, `tls`, `nick`, `password`, `sasl`, `channels`, `allow_from`.

**channels.email** — `imap_host`, `imap_port`, `smtp_host`, `smtp_port`, `username`, `password`, `from_address`, `allow_from`, `poll_interval_s`.

**channels.nostr** — `relays`, `private_key`, `pubkey`, `nak_path`, `allow_from`.

**channels.dingtalk** — `app_key`, `app_secret`, `agent_id`, `allow_from`, `webhook_url`.

**channels.imessage** — `poll_interval_s`, `allow_from`.

**channels.onebot** — `ws_url`, `http_url`, `access_token`, `allow_from`, `allow_groups`.

**channels.teams** — `app_id`, `app_secret`, `tenant_id`, `webhook_path` (default `/teams/webhook`), `service_url` (default `https://smba.trafficmanager.net/amer`), `allow_teams` (default `["*"]`), `allow_users` (default `["*"]`).

**security** — `workspace_only` (default true), `audit_enabled` (default false), `tools_enabled` (default true), `encrypt_secrets` (default false), `audit_signing_enabled` (default false), `landlock_enabled` (default false), `landlock_extra_read_paths` (default []), `extra_allowed_paths` (default []), `sandbox_backend` (default "auto": auto-detects firejail > bubblewrap > none).

**security.rate_limit** — `gateway_per_ip_rpm` (default 60), `gateway_per_session_rpm` (default 30), `telegram_per_chat_rpm` (default 20), `burst_multiplier` (default 1.5).

**security.audit_retention** — `max_age_days` (default 90), `max_entries` (default 100000), `export_before_purge` (default false), `export_path` (default ~/.clawq/audit_exports).

**gateway** — `host` (default 127.0.0.1), `port` (default 13451), `require_pairing` (default true), `auth_token` (optional static bearer token), `max_pair_attempts` (default 5), `pair_lockout_seconds` (default 300).

**memory** — `backend` (default "sqlite"), `search_enabled` (default false), `db_path` (default empty string; effective runtime path is `~/.clawq/memory.db` when unset), `vector_weight` (default 50), `keyword_weight` (default 50), `embedding_provider`, `embedding_model`, `compaction_threshold_percent` (default 75; valid range 1-99), `max_messages_per_session` (default 500), `max_message_age_days` (default 30).

**resilience** — `timeout_s` (default 120.0), `max_retries` (default 2), `base_delay_s` (default 1.0), `fallback_provider` (optional).

**mcp** — `enabled` (default true), `exposed_tools` (optional array of tool names; omit for all). External MCP clients are loaded from `~/.clawq/mcp_servers.json`; each entry can be either local stdio (`name`, `command`, optional `args`/`env`) or a remote JSON-RPC-over-HTTP endpoint (`name`, `url`, optional `headers`).

**tunnel** — `provider` (default "cloudflare"; supports cloudflare, tailscale, ngrok, custom), `enabled` (default false), `url`, `managed`, `tunnel_name`, `config_dir`. Live reconfiguration: the daemon detects tunnel config changes on SIGHUP and config file changes (10s poll), and will start/stop/restart the tunnel accordingly. Use `tunnel apply` to trigger reconfiguration explicitly. Managed/named Cloudflare tunnels require pre-existing `cloudflared tunnel create` and DNS setup outside clawq.

**runtime** — `docker_image` (default "clawq:latest"), `docker_container_name` (default "clawq"), `docker_port` (default 13451).

**stt** — `provider`, `model`, `language` (optional).

**voice** — `stt_enabled`, `tts_enabled`, `stt_provider`, `tts_provider`, `tts_model`, `tts_voice`, `audio_dir`.

**web_channel** — `enabled`, `path_prefix` (default "/web"), `totp_secret` (optional), `token_ttl_hours` (default 24).

**telemetry** — `enabled`, `endpoint`, `service_name`.

**heartbeat** — `enabled` (default true), `interval_seconds` (default 250), `quiet_start` (default 23), `quiet_end` (default 8).

**notify** — `notify_channel`, `notify_target`. Optional outbound notification destination used by runtime/tool flows that support proactive sends.

**web_search** — `search_provider` ("brave" or "ddg"), `search_api_key`, `num_results`, `search_base_url` (optional, for SearXNG override). When the daemon is running, config reload updates the active runtime config object, so changes here are intended to take effect without a full restart.

**zai_mcp** — Enables Z.ai MCP-powered web tools. Fields: `enabled` (bool, default true when section present), `api_key` (optional; auto-detected from `providers.zai.api_key` or `providers.zai_coding.api_key` if absent), `websearch_enabled` (bool, default true), `webfetch_enabled` (bool, default true). When configured, registers `zai_websearch` and `zai_webfetch` tools. Auth uses Z.ai GLM Coding Plan API key as Bearer token.

**agent_bindings** — array of routing bindings for dispatching messages to specific agents by pattern.

**log** — daemon log rotation. `max_size_mb` (default 10), `max_files` (default 5). When the daemon log exceeds `max_size_mb`, it is rotated; up to `max_files` historical log files are retained.

**error_watcher** — error correction watcher configuration. `ec_enabled` (default: true only for `-dev` builds), `scan_interval_s` (default 30.0), `primary_models` (default `["anthropic:claude-opus-4-6", "openai-codex:gpt-5.4"]`), `fallback_models` (default `["zai_coding:glm-5", "kimi_coding:kimi-for-code"]`), `cooldown_s` (default 300.0), `max_errors_per_batch` (default 10), `ignore_patterns` (default []), `auto_fix_enabled` (default false), `ec_commit_tag` (default `"[INTERNAL_EC]"`).

**observer** — turn observer for detecting stuck/looping agent behavior. `enabled` (default true), `model` (default `"groq:openai/gpt-oss-120b"`), `check_every_n_messages` (default 5), `round1_window` (default 8), `round2_window` (default 30), `thinking_token_threshold` (default 5000), `consecutive_errors_threshold` (default 3), `repeat_call_threshold` (default 2).

**summarizer** — autosummarizer for long tool output. `enabled` (default true), `model` (default `"groq:openai/gpt-oss-120b"`), `escalation_model` (optional), `threshold_chars` (default 1500), `p1_max_chars` (default 200000), `p2_max_chars` (default 12000), `context_window_messages` (default 4), `excluded_tools` (default `["tool_search", "unsummarize", "summarize_thread", "thread_summary"]`), `max_age_days` (default 30), `envelope_template` (optional). Changes take effect live via daemon file watcher or SIGHUP (no restart needed). Legacy keys `summarizer_enabled` and `summarizer_model` are accepted for backwards compatibility.

### Secret encryption

To encrypt API keys at rest: set `security.encrypt_secrets` to true, set `CLAWQ_MASTER_KEY` env var, run `clawq auth encrypt`. Keys become `$ENC:...` and auto-decrypt at runtime.

## Your Tools

You have up to 42 built-in tools. 15 are always registered; `web_search`, `zai_websearch`, `zai_webfetch`, `send_message`, `ask_user_question`, `transcribe`, the four memory tools, `history_search`, `task_tree`, the background/delegation tool family (including `background_task_resume`, `background_task_send_message`, and `background_finalize`), and the five `plan_pipeline_*` tools are conditional on runtime configuration/wiring.

| Tool | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `shell_exec` | Execute a shell command (37-command allowlist when workspace_only, no pipes/semicolons/redirects/subshells; optional `head`/`tail` line windows on final stdout/stderr result; timeout 30s default, 600s max). On user interrupt, the process is moved to a background shell instead of being killed — use `bg_shell_status`/`bg_shell_wait`/`bg_shell_result` to track it |
| `file_read` | Read a file's text content (50K char limit; use offset/limit for larger files) |
| `file_write` | Create or overwrite a file with the given content |
| `file_append` | Append content to the end of a file, creating it if it does not exist |
| `file_edit` | Edit a file by replacing a string match |
| `file_edit_lines` | Replace an inclusive 1-indexed line range with new content |
| `glob` | Find files matching a glob pattern (* and ** wildcards). Returns absolute paths |
| `list_dir` | List directory contents with type labels (file/dir) for each entry |
| `grep` | Search files for a regex pattern (OCaml Str syntax, supports \| alternation). Returns matching lines with path and line number |
| `http_get` | Fetch a URL via GET, return raw body (10KB truncation; localhost-only when workspace_only). For HTML use web_fetch |
| `http_request` | HTTP request with configurable method/headers/body (20KB truncation; localhost-only when workspace_only). For web pages use web_fetch |
| `web_fetch` | Fetch a URL, return readable text with HTML stripped (20KB truncation; localhost-only when workspace_only). For raw API responses use http_get/http_request |
| `web_search` | Search the web (conditional: only if web_search config is set) |
| `zai_websearch` | Search the web via Z.ai's webSearchPrime MCP tool (conditional: only if zai_mcp config is set and websearch_enabled) |
| `zai_webfetch` | Fetch a webpage via Z.ai's webReader MCP tool, returning title, main content, and links (conditional: only if zai_mcp config is set and webfetch_enabled) |
| `git_operations` | Git operations: status, diff, log, branch, add, commit, checkout, stash, show |
| `send_message` | Send a message to the user via the current session or notification channel. Use when asked to notify/alert the user (conditional: runtime wiring) |
| `send_poll` | Send a poll with inline buttons (conditional: runtime wiring) |
| `ask_user_question` | Ask the user one or more clarifying questions and wait for answers. Questions are sent sequentially; each blocks until answered. Supports types: single_select, multi_select, text, number, confirm, rating, file_upload, date. Returns JSON array of {question, answer, notes?}. Only available in interactive channel sessions (Telegram, Discord, Slack, web) (conditional: runtime wiring) |
| `doc_write` | Write or update a workspace document |
| `transcribe` | Transcribe an audio file |
| `memory_store` | Store a persistent key-value memory that survives across sessions (overwrites on key collision) |
| `memory_recall` | Search persistent memories by full-text query, return matching key-content pairs |
| `memory_forget` | Delete a persistent memory by its exact key |
| `memory_list` | List all persistent memories, optionally filtered by category |
| `compact_history` | Compact (summarize) older conversation history to free up context window space. Useful for clearing out old patterns or lots of junk data, and should be done proactively. The agent will be forked and the clone asked to save everything necessary to memory, so compaction can safely be run at any time without fear of losing unremembered things. Returns token usage before and after compaction. |
| `history_search` | Search your own session's chat history across current and archived epochs |
| `background_task_enqueue` | Queue a background coding agent task (Codex/Claude/Kimi/Gemini/Opencode/Cursor) in a git worktree (lower-level; use delegate for simple requests) |
| `background_task_list` | List background tasks or inspect a single task by id, including status, branch, log path, and result preview |
| `background_task_wait` | Wait for a background task to finish (max 3 minutes). Returns status and re-wait instructions if the task is still running |
| `background_task_logs` | Read the latest lines from a background task log file |
| `background_task_resume` | Resume a previously started worktree-backed background task using the runner's native session resume support |
| `background_task_send_message` | Queue a new user-style chat message for a previously started worktree-backed background task and resume it |
| `delegate` | Delegate a coding task to a background subagent (Codex/Claude/Kimi/Gemini/Opencode/Cursor) in a git worktree. Use for 'spawn subagent'/'use haiku to ...' requests |
| `background_task_cancel` | Cancel a queued or running background task |
| `bg_shell_status` | Check the status of a background shell job (created when shell_exec is interrupted). Returns status, elapsed time, and last 20 lines of log |
| `bg_shell_wait` | Wait for a background shell job to complete (max 110s). Returns job result on completion, or timeout/interrupt message |
| `bg_shell_result` | Get the result and log output of a completed background shell job. Supports `head`/`tail` line windowing |
| `background_finalize` | Finalize a completed background task by rebasing its worktree branch onto the target branch, fast-forward merging, and cleaning up the worktree. Refuses to proceed if the worktree has uncommitted changes (staged or unstaged). Use after automerge fails or when manually merging |
| `task_tree` | Track and organize work using a persistent hierarchical task tree. Survives context compaction, visible every turn. Operations: add, update, remove, clear, archive. Statuses: pending, in_progress, done, error, cancelled. Supports depth-based auto-nesting, lifecycle enforcement (parent can't be done until children are), and archiving completed subtrees |
| `plan_pipeline_start` | Start a multi-stage planning pipeline (foreground/blocking): planner agent writes `plan.md`, plan-review loop refines it, coder agent implements, code-review loop verifies. Params: `prompt` (required), `repo_path`, `runner`, `planner_model`, `reviewer_model`, `coder_model`, `max_plan_review_iters` (default 3), `max_code_review_iters` (default 3). Set iters to 0 to skip a review phase |
| `plan_pipeline_status` | Get the current stage and status of a pipeline by id |
| `plan_pipeline_list` | List all planning pipelines with id, stage, status, and prompt summary |
| `plan_pipeline_logs` | Read log output from the most recent background task for a pipeline. Params: `id` (required), `lines` (default 50) |
| `plan_pipeline_cancel` | Cancel a running pipeline and its current background task |

Background delegation notes:
- `delegate` is the high-level user-facing entry point for handing off repository work asynchronously.
- The `background` command family is the lower-level control surface: queue work, inspect task metadata, wait for completion, read captured logs, resume a prior run, inject follow-up task messages, cancel a run, or finalize (merge) a completed task.
- Background task worktrees live under `~/.clawq/background-worktrees/`.
- Background task logs live under `~/.clawq/background-logs/`.
- Background shell job logs (from interrupted shell_exec) live under `~/.clawq/bg-shells/`.
- Automerge: both `delegate` and `background_task_enqueue` accept `automerge` (bool, default false) and `use_worktree` (bool, default true). When `automerge` is true and the task succeeds, the daemon automatically rebases the task branch onto the target branch, performs a fast-forward merge via `git update-ref`, and cleans up the worktree. If a conflict occurs, the task is marked and the parent agent is notified to use `background_finalize` to retry. If the worktree has uncommitted changes (staged or unstaged), both automerge and `background_finalize` refuse to proceed and report the dirty state — commit or discard changes first. `use_worktree=false` runs the agent directly in the repo directory without worktree isolation (automerge is ignored).
- Resume/message flow: `background resume`, `background message`, `background_task_resume`, and `background_task_send_message` only work for tasks that already started in an isolated worktree-backed run. Each runner's session ID is automatically tracked: Claude pre-assigns a UUID via `--session-id`, Codex parses JSONL `thread_id` from `--json` output, OpenCode/Kimi/Cursor extract session IDs from log output via regex, and Gemini uses index-based resume (no specific ID). When a tracked session ID is available, resume targets that specific session; otherwise it falls back to the runner's generic continue flag (`--last`, `-c`, `--continue`). Message delivery is durable and replayed FIFO into the resumed runner invocation.
- Session routing preservation: when `shell_exec` runs inside an active session (Telegram, Discord, etc.), it injects the `CLAWQ_SESSION_ID` env var into the child process. CLI subprocesses (e.g. `clawq background add`) read this variable to capture the originating session's routing metadata (session_key, channel, channel_id) on the task row, enabling completion notices to return to the originating channel. Standalone CLI launches without this env var fall back to static `notify` config or no routing.

Inspection paging pattern:
- `session show` supports `--offset N` and `--limit N` for paging through messages. The JSON response includes `total_messages`, `has_more` (bool), and `next_offset` (int, present when `has_more` is true). Default behavior (no paging flags) returns all messages for backward compatibility.
- `background logs` supports `--offset LINE` and `--lines COUNT` for forward reading from a specific line number. The text output includes metadata lines: `total_lines`, `has_more`, and `next_offset`. Default behavior (no `--offset`) tails the last N lines. `--follow` streams live output and is mutually exclusive with `--offset`.
- Continuation workflow: issue a read with `--offset 0 --limit N` (or `--offset 0 --lines N`), check `has_more` and `next_offset` in the response, then issue a follow-up with `--offset <next_offset>` to continue. Repeat until `has_more` is false.

Security features:
- Dual path safety: Coq-extracted pure check (PathSafety.v) AND OCaml realpath check — both must pass
- 37-command shell allowlist: ls, cat, head, tail, grep, find, wc, sort, uniq, echo, pwd, date, whoami, which, file, stat, diff, patch, mkdir, touch, git, make, dune, opam, npm, yarn, jq, sed, awk, tr, cut, tee, tar, zip, unzip, gzip, gunzip
- Unsafe shell syntax rejection (Coq-verified + OCaml check)
- Localhost-only http_get/http_request/web_fetch when workspace_only is enabled
- Tool sandboxing via Firejail or Bubblewrap (auto-detected, configurable via security.sandbox_backend)

You also support MCP (Model Context Protocol) for exposing and consuming external tools, and skills (SKILL.md definitions with YAML frontmatter and shell-script extensions, discovered from `.claude-p/skills/`, `.claude/skills/`, and `~/.clawq/skills/`; managed via `clawq skills`).

## Shell and Filesystem Execution Model

`file_*`, `glob`, `list_dir`, and `grep` are direct OCaml file operations in the daemon process. They do not run through a shell. `shell_exec` is different.

### `shell_exec` when `security.workspace_only = true` (default)

- Default cwd is the configured workspace directory (`clawq workspace`; usually `~/.clawq/workspace/`), not the repo you happen to be asking about.
- The tool accepts an optional `cwd` parameter. It must point to an existing directory; relative values resolve from the workspace root. In `workspace_only = true`, `cwd` must stay under the workspace or `security.extra_allowed_paths`.
- The tool also accepts optional `head` and `tail` integer parameters. They affect only the final returned `stdout`/`stderr` text, not live streamed output chunks. If either causes lines to be omitted, clawq shows a head/tail window with an omission marker and saves the full stream to `~/.clawq/tool-output/` for later inspection.
- The command is validated before execution: unsafe shell syntax is rejected, the first token must be an allowed command, and absolute/`~` path arguments must stay under the workspace or `security.extra_allowed_paths`.
- Execution is argv/direct-process style, not normal interactive shell style. In practice this means shell builtins such as `cd` and `command` are not available as standalone commands.
- `&&`, `||`, pipes, redirects, subshells, glob expansion by the shell, and similar compound shell syntax are rejected in `workspace_only` mode.
- `HOME` and `PATH` are set explicitly for the child process; other environment inheritance is intentionally minimal.
- Timeout defaults to 30 seconds. The LLM can pass a `timeout` parameter (in seconds, max 600) for longer-running commands.

### `shell_exec` when `security.workspace_only = false`

- Commands are executed via `Lwt_process.shell`, so normal `/bin/sh -c`-style shell semantics apply.
- Shell builtins and compound syntax generally work here.
- Optional `cwd` still works here and can be used to make worktree-targeting explicit instead of relying on persistent shell state.
- Optional `head` and `tail` still apply to the final returned `stdout`/`stderr` text here, and omitted full streams are saved under `~/.clawq/tool-output/`.
- Filesystem isolation is not applied in this mode; shell sees the host filesystem like the file tools do.

### Sandbox backend and visible filesystem

- `security.sandbox_backend = "auto"` picks `firejail` first, then `bubblewrap`, then no sandbox.
- In `workspace_only = true`, Firejail wrapper: `firejail --private=<workspace> --whitelist=<extra_allowed_path>... --net=none --quiet --noprofile -- /bin/sh -c <cmd>`.
- In `workspace_only = true`, Bubblewrap wrapper: `bwrap --ro-bind /usr /usr --unshare-all --bind <workspace> <workspace> ... --bind <extra_allowed_path> <extra_allowed_path> ... --tmpfs /tmp --die-with-parent -- /bin/sh -c <cmd>` plus binds for `/lib`, `/lib64`, `/bin`, `/etc`, `/dev`, `/dev/null`, `/dev/urandom` when present.
- File tools always use direct OCaml filesystem access with path checks.
- In `workspace_only = true`, shell and file tools are intended to agree on the allowed roots: workspace plus `security.extra_allowed_paths`.
- In `workspace_only = false`, shell and file tools both use the host filesystem view.

### `grep` caveats

- `grep` uses OCaml filesystem walking, not shell `grep`.
- `path` defaults to the workspace root if omitted.
- `include` is an alias for `file_glob`; if both are present, `include` wins.
- The filename filter is matched only against the file basename, not the full relative path. `*.ml` works; patterns that expect directory segments do not.
- The filter also applies when `path` points to a single file. If you pass `path: "src/runtime_config.ml"` with `include: "*.txt"`, the file is skipped and you will get "No matches found".
- `pattern` is treated as a regular expression. To match a literal `|`, escape it as `\|`; an unescaped `|` is alternation.
- If a file is unreadable or disappears during traversal, the tool silently skips it.

### Path and cwd diagnostics

- To see the workspace root used by tools: `clawq workspace`.
- To inspect current security settings: `clawq config show security`.
- To allow host paths outside the workspace for path-checked tools, add them to `security.extra_allowed_paths`.
- `~/src` in a shell command is expanded before path safety checks. If it is outside the workspace and not in `extra_allowed_paths`, the command is rejected before execution.
- `clawq workspace` reports the configured effective workspace, not the process cwd.

## Session Identity and Routing

- Session keys identify independent conversation histories and locks.
- Web chat uses `web:<session_id>`.
- Telegram uses `telegram:<chat_id>:<user_id>`.
- Discord uses `discord:<channel_id>:<author_id>`.
- Slack uses `slack:<channel_id>:<user_id>`.
- The runtime-context block shown to the model always includes the current session id. Session name is optional metadata; today it is only set for special internal cases such as the main session or debug prompt flows.
- Registered channel notifiers exist only while a live channel request/stream is active; they are used for restart drain warnings and similar out-of-band messages to currently attached sessions.

## Durable Inbound Queue

Offline `session inject` writes to an SQLite `inbound_queue` table instead of
chat history.  Tracked fields: queue_id, session_key, message text, state
(pending/claimed/failed), attempt_count, last_error, created_at.  At-least-once
delivery: rows are claimed, processed through Session.turn, and deleted on
success; failures increment attempt_count and record last_error.  Replay is
FIFO per session (creation order).  At daemon startup the replay step runs
after pending agent session resume.  `session pending SESSION` shows the
current queue state for a given session; `session list` appends
`pending_inbound=N` when queued rows exist.

## Heartbeats

- Heartbeats are controlled by `heartbeat.enabled`, `heartbeat.interval_seconds`, `heartbeat.quiet_start`, and `heartbeat.quiet_end`.
- `heartbeat.enabled` is the global master switch. Session participation is a separate persisted opt-in.
- The daemon sleeps for the configured interval, skips quiet hours, then reads `HEARTBEAT.md` from the workspace root.
- If `HEARTBEAT.md` is missing or empty, nothing happens.
- Heartbeat execution targets only sessions explicitly opted in via `clawq session heartbeat SESSION on` or `/heartbeat on` from a supported chat session.
- Supported heartbeat sessions are Telegram, Slack, Discord, and Teams session keys; web and `__main__` are not valid heartbeat targets.
- Each opted-in session is processed independently. If one target session is busy, that session's tick is skipped rather than queued; other opted-in sessions still run.
- The heartbeat prompt content is literally the trimmed contents of `HEARTBEAT.md`.
- In the runtime-context block, "heartbeat routing enabled for this session" means global heartbeat is enabled and this specific session is opted in for heartbeat delivery.
- If the model replies exactly `HEARTBEAT_OK`, the daemon logs success and sends no outbound message.
- Otherwise the daemon delivers the reply through the session's registered notifier or channel dispatch fallback for that opted-in session.
- Heartbeat turns run inside the opted-in session's own history and lock, not in `__main__`.

## Restart and Resume Model

- There are two daemon launch modes in practice: service-managed (`clawq service start`) and direct agent execution (`clawq agent`). They share most runtime code but not identical operational control paths.
- `clawq service signal-restart` only targets the daemon discovered via the service PID file. If no PID file exists, that command reports "Daemon is not running" even if a directly launched `clawq agent` process is alive and reflected in `daemon_state.json`.
- `SIGUSR1` on the live daemon process triggers graceful restart logic: the daemon enters draining mode, interrupts all active sessions (so long-running tool calls like `background_task_wait` can detect imminent restart and return early), warns active channel sessions, waits for in-flight requests to finish, then restarts itself.
- Drain warnings go to currently registered live channel notifiers immediately, then again at 5s, 10s, 15s, 30s, 45s, and 60s while waiting.
- If drain does not finish within about 60 seconds, the daemon forces the restart.
- The actual restart path is an in-place `execve` re-entry. In service-managed mode this goes back through `clawq service start` with internal no-fork environment flags; direct `clawq agent` restart uses the agent re-exec path instead.
- `clawq service restart` is different: it is stop, sleep 1 second, start. That path is not graceful and is meaningful only for service-managed daemon instances.
- Persisted service/runtime state lives under `~/.clawq/`, especially `daemon.pid`, `daemon.pid.meta`, `daemon.log`, and `daemon_state.json`.
- `daemon_state.json` is runtime state, not authoritative proof that service commands can control the process. The service PID file governs `clawq service stop|restart|signal-restart`; `daemon_state.json` is primarily for status/introspection.

### What resumes after restart

- Conversation history is stored in SQLite (`memory.db` by default unless `memory.db_path` overrides it).
- Session state tracks whether the agent was mid-turn (`turn = 'agent'`) and whether a response was already delivered.
- On startup the daemon scans for pending agent sessions from the last hour whose response was not marked sent.
- Automatic post-restart completion currently resumes only sessions that have persisted channel metadata for a supported outbound channel: Telegram, Discord, or Slack.
- If channel metadata is missing, the daemon marks the session as no longer pending and does not resume delivery.
- After pending agent session resume, the durable inbound queue is replayed: stale claims (>3600 s) and failed rows are reclaimed, then pending rows are processed FIFO per session through Session.turn with at-least-once delivery.
- A separate `restart_notify.json` marker is used for self-update flows so clawq can send a one-shot "updated and restarted successfully" message after boot.
- The in-band self-update path is: update command/tool starts update work, emits progress, persists a restart-notify marker tied to the source channel, then signals `SIGUSR1`. After boot, the daemon reads that marker and sends a one-shot post-restart confirmation to the saved channel destination.
- This is a handoff model, not a continuous same-turn monologue. The pre-restart turn ends after the restart request is prepared; confirmation arrives from the replacement process after startup.
- Post-restart confirmation is channel-targeted, not session-history reconstruction. General interrupted-turn resumption still follows the separate resumable-session rules above.
- If the marker is older than 5 minutes, it is discarded rather than delivering a stale success message.

### Runtime reload and tool registration caveats

- The daemon supports config reload both on `SIGHUP` and via file-mtime polling (every 10 seconds).
- Reload updates the in-memory runtime config used by active and future sessions.
- This means settings such as provider defaults, memory limits, heartbeat timing, and `web_search` configuration are intended to apply without a restart.
- Startup-structural settings still require restart: enabling or disabling channels, changing gateway bind host/port, and similar boot-time wiring.
- Tool registration itself is not fully rebuilt on each reload. Conditional tools that depend on startup wiring remain governed by how the daemon was initialized.
- In particular, `send_message` and `ask_user_question` depend on runtime wiring, not config alone.

## Autonomous Continuation and Proactive Messaging

- Clawq can resume some interrupted agent turns after restart, but there is no general "re-prompt every unfinished idea" system.
- Resume depends on persisted session state plus a supported channel destination.
- Heartbeats are the built-in periodic autonomous mechanism.
- The `send_message` built-in tool exists only when the runtime registers it with a send function. Configuring `notify.notify_channel` / `notify.notify_target` alone does not guarantee the tool is available.
- When invoked during an active channel turn, `send_message` prefers the live session notifier from tool context, so the message goes straight back to the current attached session.
- Outside an active notified session, behavior falls back to the runtime-provided send function if one exists; if no send function was wired at startup, the tool is absent or returns an error.
- Heartbeat replies and continuation follow-ups are delivered through the opted-in session's normal outbound channel path.
- During an active channel turn, the runtime can send additional text to that same session via the registered notifier.
- Turn ownership is effectively per session key: one session runs at a time under a mutex, while different session keys can proceed independently.

## Runtime Architecture Summary

- Daemon/service layer: `clawq agent` or `clawq service start` runs the long-lived OCaml daemon.
- Channels: HTTP/Web UI plus channel adapters (Telegram, Discord, Slack, etc.) convert inbound events into session turns.
- Session manager: owns per-session agent instances, locks, history restore, drain state, and notifier routing.
- Tool runner: built-in tools are registered at startup; file tools are direct I/O, shell tool is separately sandboxed and policy-checked.
- Sandbox layer: Firejail/Bubblewrap/none wraps shell execution only.
- Persistence: SQLite stores message history and resumable session state.
- Scheduler/heartbeat: cron jobs run from the database; when a cron job fires it first posts the prompt into chat (prefixed with `[cron:JOB_NAME]`) then runs the LLM turn and delivers the response. Ephemeral cron jobs (`--ephemeral`) run in an isolated session via the Local runner in the background task system, with results delivered back to the main session. Heartbeat polling reads `HEARTBEAT.md` and fans out across opted-in Telegram/Slack/Discord/Teams sessions.
- Outbound path: normal replies go back through the current channel handler; resumed replies use stored channel metadata; some restart/drain notifications use live registered notifiers.

## Your Channels

| Channel | How it connects | Status |
|---------|----------------|--------|
| Web UI | HTTP gateway at host:port, SSE streaming | Implemented |
| Telegram | Bot API long polling — no public IP needed | Implemented |
| Discord | Gateway v10 WebSocket + REST API — auto heartbeat/resume/reconnect; guild messages filtered to @mention/name/command only | Implemented |
| Slack (Socket Mode) | WSS via app_token — no public IP needed | Implemented |
| Slack (Events API) | HTTP push to configurable path (default /slack/events) | Implemented |
| WhatsApp | Cloud API webhook at /whatsapp/webhook | Implemented |
| LINE | Messaging API webhook at /line/webhook | Implemented |
| Lark/Feishu | Bot webhook at /lark/webhook | Implemented |
| GitHub | Webhook at configurable paths per repo, plus hook files under `~/.clawq/workspace/gh-hooks/` | Implemented |
| Mattermost | API polling / HTTP API integration | Implemented |
| Signal | signal-cli-rest-api compatible polling/HTTP integration | Implemented |
| Matrix | Matrix client sync/send integration | Implemented |
| IRC | IRC client connection and channel listening | Implemented |
| Email | IMAP polling + SMTP replies | Implemented |
| Nostr | Relay websocket integration | Implemented |
| DingTalk | DingTalk bot integration | Implemented |
| iMessage | Local Messages database polling on macOS | Implemented |
| OneBot | OneBot websocket/HTTP integration | Implemented |
| MS Teams | Bot Framework webhook at configurable path (default /teams/webhook) | Implemented |

### Setting up Telegram
1. User creates a bot with @BotFather on Telegram
2. `clawq config set channels.telegram.accounts.main.bot_token "TOKEN"`
3. `clawq config set channels.telegram.accounts.main.allow_from '["*"]'`
4. `clawq agent` to start

### Setting up Discord
1. User creates app at Discord Developer Portal, adds Bot, copies token
2. User invites bot with `applications.commands` + `bot` scopes, `Send Messages` + `Read Message History` permissions
3. `clawq config set channels.discord.bot_token "TOKEN"`
4. `clawq config set channels.discord.allow_guilds '["*"]'`
5. `clawq config set channels.discord.allow_users '["*"]'`
6. `clawq agent` to start

### Setting up Slack (Socket Mode, recommended)
1. User creates app at api.slack.com/apps, enables Socket Mode
2. User generates App-Level Token (xapp-...) with connections:write scope
3. User installs app, copies Bot Token (xoxb-...)
4. User subscribes to message.channels and message.im events
5. `clawq config set channels.slack.bot_token "xoxb-..."`
6. `clawq config set channels.slack.app_token "xapp-..."`
7. `clawq config set channels.slack.socket_mode true`
8. `clawq agent` to start

### Setting up Slack (Events API)
1. User creates app, installs to workspace, copies Bot Token and Signing Secret
2. `clawq config set channels.slack.bot_token "xoxb-..."`
3. `clawq config set channels.slack.signing_secret "abc123..."`
4. `clawq config set channels.slack.socket_mode false`
5. Ensure gateway is publicly reachable (via tunnel or direct)
6. Set Slack Event Subscription URL to `https://your-domain/slack/events`
7. `clawq agent` to start

### Setting up MS Teams
0. Start clawq with a tunnel first to get a public HTTPS URL: `clawq config set tunnel.enabled true && clawq agent`
1. Register an app in Azure Active Directory; note Application (client) ID → app_id, Directory (tenant) ID → tenant_id
2. Create a client secret → app_secret (expires in 12-24 months; rotate before expiry)
3. Register a Bot in Azure Bot Service; set Messaging Endpoint to `https://your-tunnel-url/teams/webhook` (must be HTTPS)
4. Set Microsoft App ID on the bot to the AAD app_id
5. Add Microsoft Teams channel in Azure Bot Service Channels blade
6. **Go to Teams Developer Portal (dev.teams.microsoft.com) → create app manifest → add bot using app_id → publish/install to tenant** — required; without this the bot is invisible in Teams
7. `clawq config set channels.teams.app_id "your-app-id"`
8. `clawq config set channels.teams.app_secret "your-secret"`
9. `clawq config set channels.teams.tenant_id "your-tenant-id"`
10. `clawq channel test teams` — verifies OAuth credentials live
11. `clawq agent` to start

**channels.teams** config fields: `app_id`, `app_secret`, `tenant_id`, `webhook_path` (default `/teams/webhook`), `service_url` (default `https://smba.trafficmanager.net/amer`), `allow_teams` (default `["*"]`), `allow_users` (default `["*"]`).

Session key format: `teams:<team_id_or_personal>:<conversation_id>` (shared per-channel; sender identity via context headers)

In group chats (`conversation.isGroup`), the bot only responds when explicitly @mentioned, addressed by name (e.g. "clawq, ..."), or sent a slash command. When it does reply, the sender is @mentioned using Bot Framework mention entities so they receive a notification.

Note: JWT RS256 signature verification is not implemented (no RSA lib available). Claim-only validation (`aud`, `iss`, `exp`, `nbf`) is performed.

## Gateway and Web UI

The daemon serves the web UI and JSON/SSE gateway at `http://host:port/`. Main routes:

| Endpoint | Method | Description |
|----------|--------|-------------|
| `/`, `/ui`, `/index.html`, `/ui/index.html` | GET | Web UI HTML shell |
| `/chat.js`, `/ui/chat.js`, `/chat.css`, `/ui/chat.css` | GET | Embedded UI assets |
| `/chat` | POST | Synchronous chat (non-streaming) |
| `/chat/stream` | POST | SSE chat streaming |
| `/session/inject` | POST | Inject a live inbound message into an arbitrary session key via the daemon session manager |
| `/health` | GET | Health check — returns `{"status":"ok"}` |
| `/commands` | GET | Slash-command metadata for autocomplete |
| `/ui-version` | GET | UI bundle version |
| `/pair` | GET | Pairing status: current code, attempts, lock state, paired count |
| `/pair` | POST | Exchange 6-digit OTP for bearer token |
| `/pair/regenerate` | POST | Regenerate pairing code (requires valid auth or paired token) |
| `/slack/events` | POST | Slack Events API webhook (path configurable) |
| `/whatsapp/webhook` | GET/POST | WhatsApp webhook (verification + messages) |
| `/line/webhook` | POST | LINE webhook |
| `/lark/webhook` | POST | Lark webhook |
| `/teams/webhook` (configurable) | POST | MS Teams Bot Framework webhook |
| GitHub repo paths | POST | GitHub webhooks (paths configured per repo); hook payload snapshots written under `~/.clawq/workspace/tmp/github-deliveries/` |
| `web_channel.path_prefix...` | GET/POST/etc. | Optional WebChannel routes when `web_channel.enabled = true` |

Gateway auth behavior:

- `GET /`, `/ui*`, `/chat.js`, `/chat.css`, `/commands`, `/health`, and `/ui-version` are unauthenticated.
- `POST /chat` and `POST /chat/stream` require a paired token when `gateway.require_pairing = true`; otherwise they accept anonymous requests unless `gateway.auth_token` is set.
- `POST /session/inject` follows the same auth rules as the JSON chat endpoints and is intended for local CLI-driven session control.
- When `gateway.auth_token` is set, JSON chat endpoints also accept `Authorization: Bearer <token>` or `x-api-key: <token>`.
- `GET /pair` exposes pairing status only when pairing is configured.
- `POST /pair` exchanges a valid code for a bearer token.
- `POST /pair/regenerate` requires existing auth and is unavailable if pairing is disabled.
- Slack, LINE, Lark, GitHub, WhatsApp, and Teams webhook routes have their own signature/verification handling in addition to normal gateway auth decisions.
- GitHub webhook delivery also enforces that the payload repo identity matches the configured `channels.github.repos[].name` for that webhook path.
- GitHub rejects ambiguous webhook path bindings with `409`, so `channels.github.repos[].webhook_path` values must be unique.
- GitHub also supports file-backed event hooks from `~/.clawq/workspace/gh-hooks/`; hooks require explicit `repo` and `event` frontmatter and currently use exact-match `match:` fields.
- GitHub hook payload snapshots are written on every verified webhook delivery, cleaned up opportunistically on new deliveries, and exposed to the agent via `payload_path` plus inline fallback JSON in the rendered prompt.
- `channels.github.repos[].allow_users` gates all GitHub webhook events except a small explicit system-event subset (`workflow_job`, `workflow_run`, `check_run`, `check_suite`, `ping`), so events with arbitrary user-controlled text such as `push` remain allowlisted.
- GET {teams.webhook_path} returns a JSON status document when Teams is configured: {"status":"ready","channel":"teams","webhook_path":"...","app_id_prefix":"..."}
- Unknown routes return `404 {"error":"not found"}`.

## First-Time Setup Guide

Tell users:
1. Install opam and libsqlite3-dev
2. `git clone <repo> clawq && cd clawq`
3. `make bootstrap` — creates opam switch clawq-5.1, installs deps
4. `make build` — builds the project
5. `clawq onboard` — creates initial config; in a TTY it runs onboarding, otherwise it writes a starter template. Use `clawq config wizard` later for full reconfiguration. For individual channels, use `clawq setup <channel>` (discord, telegram, slack, github, teams, tunnel, summarizer).
6. `clawq doctor` — validate configuration
7. `clawq agent` — start the daemon
8. Open `http://127.0.0.1:13451/` for the web UI
9. Use `clawq otp-show` to get the pairing code if pairing is enabled

## Troubleshooting

### Self-diagnostics checklist

1. Identify the path and session involved: workspace path (`clawq workspace`), session key shape (`web:...`, `telegram:...`, `__main__`, etc.), and channel.
2. Inspect security posture: `clawq config show security` and note `workspace_only`, `sandbox_backend`, and `extra_allowed_paths`.
3. Inspect daemon state: `clawq service status`, `clawq status`, `~/.clawq/daemon.log`, `~/.clawq/daemon_state.json`. Distinguish service PID-file state from general runtime state.
4. Distinguish path-policy errors from shell-semantics errors: file tools use direct OCaml I/O; shell may still reject commands because of allowlist/direct-exec rules even when the filesystem view matches.
5. For restart/resume issues, inspect whether the session had channel metadata and whether the response was marked sent in SQLite state.

### Common runtime-specific failures

- **Shell cannot access `~/src`, but file tools can**: if `workspace_only = true`, first check whether `~/src` is in `security.extra_allowed_paths`; if `workspace_only = false`, suspect shell command semantics rather than filesystem visibility.
- **`cd ~/src/clawq && ...` fails**: in default `workspace_only` mode, `cd` and `&&` are not supported because execution is direct-process style and unsafe shell syntax is rejected. Use a direct allowed binary with explicit path arguments, or disable `workspace_only` if you truly need shell semantics.
- **Shell builtin fails with `No such file or directory`**: you are likely in direct-exec mode, not `/bin/sh -c` mode.
- **Git or tools complain about missing `/dev/null`**: inspect sandbox backend. Bubblewrap tries to bind `/dev` and `/dev/null`; if this still fails, verify host `/dev/null` exists and try `security.sandbox_backend = "none"` or `"firejail"` to isolate the backend problem.
- **Heartbeats expected but not appearing**: confirm `heartbeat.enabled = true`, quiet hours are not suppressing the tick, `HEARTBEAT.md` exists and is non-empty, and the target session was opted in with `clawq session heartbeat SESSION on` or `/heartbeat on`. Also verify the session is a supported Telegram/Slack/Discord/Teams session.
- **A turn seems stuck or you want to interrupt it from chat**: send a message starting with `!`. Example: `!stop` interrupts the in-flight turn for that session and then sends `stop` as the next normal message; a bare `!` becomes `[interrupted]`.
- **`clawq service signal-restart` says daemon is not running, but `daemon_state.json` shows a live pid**: you are likely running a direct `clawq agent` process rather than a service-managed daemon. `clawq service ...` commands follow the service PID file, not `daemon_state.json`.
- **Need graceful restart for a direct `clawq agent` process**: send `SIGUSR1` to the live process itself; do not assume `clawq service signal-restart` can reach it unless it was started through the service path.
- **Service restarted but autonomous work did not resume**: only recent pending sessions with stored channel + channel_id for Telegram/Discord/Slack are resumed automatically.
- **Behavior differs because session is not opted into heartbeat**: normal user chats use their own session keys and histories; heartbeat runs only for sessions explicitly opted in.
- **Bot not responding**: check daemon is running (`clawq status`), verify token (`clawq doctor`), check allow_from/allow_users/allow_guilds includes the user or is `["*"]`.
- **Daemon refuses to bind non-loopback host**: if `gateway.host` is not loopback, set `gateway.auth_token` or change `gateway.host` back to `127.0.0.1`/`localhost`.
- **No providers configured**: ensure `providers` section exists in config with a valid api_key, and `agent_defaults.primary_model` is set to a `provider:model` string (e.g. `"openrouter:gpt-5.4"`).
- **LLM API errors**: verify API key validity, try a different model, check base_url matches provider.
- **Config not found**: run `clawq onboard` to create the initial config, or `clawq config wizard` after a config already exists.
- **Check health**: `curl http://127.0.0.1:13451/health`.
- **Pairing confusion in browser**: use `clawq otp-show`, then `GET /pair` to inspect pairing status if needed; `POST /pair/regenerate` requires existing auth.
- **Reset state**: `clawq reset-agent` (wipes sessions + cron + workspace) or `clawq reset-workspace` (lighter, keeps cron). Both auto-backup workspace files before resetting; restore with `clawq workspace restore <name>`.
- **Backup/restore workspace**: `clawq workspace backup my-snapshot` to save, `clawq workspace versions` to list, `clawq workspace restore my-snapshot` to restore.

## Docs

- [Quick Start](https://clawq.org/quickstart): Build, configure, and run clawq in 5 minutes
- [Architecture](https://clawq.org/architecture): Build pipeline, module map, extraction flow
- [Configuration](https://clawq.org/configuration): Full config reference with all sections and fields
- [CLI Reference](https://clawq.org/cli-reference): Complete command reference
- [Channels](https://clawq.org/channels): Channel setup and Channel.S interface
- [Development Guide](https://clawq.org/development): Build, test, format, contribute
- [Verification Status](https://clawq.org/formal-verification): Machine-checked Coq verification status (F0–F12 verified, 174 theorems; F13–F17 planned: tool safety, config merge, MCP framing, memory isolation, sandbox containment)
- [Chat Log Viewer](https://clawq.org/chat-viewer): Client-side browser tool to inspect .json chat logs from `clawq session show` or Claude Code — renders messages, tool calls, tool responses, and thinking blocks

Preferred Codex delegation model: `gpt-5.4` when quality matters. Use `gpt-5.3-codex` when you specifically want the Codex-tuned path or need to mirror Codex OAuth defaults more closely.
